text.title: Design Tokens Basics Core Rules
text.summary: 7 testable rules grounding the Design Tokens Basics methodology. Each pairs a binary check with a sourced rationale; the last rule binds the decision tree skip path.

rule.statement: Spec declares the canonical token categories: color, spacing, typography, radius, shadow, motion. Inventing custom categories before these six are filled is rejected.
rule.rationale: Industry consensus (W3C Design Tokens CG); custom categories sprout when the canonical six are unfilled.

rule.statement: Spec has TWO layers: base tokens (e.g. `color.blue.500`) and alias tokens (e.g. `color.action.primary`). Components consume aliases; aliases reference bases.
rule.rationale: Renaming a brand colour without rewriting components requires the alias layer.

rule.statement: Naming convention is one of: `category.subcategory.scale` OR `category-subcategory-scale`. Mixing both is rejected.
ONE file is source of truth (e.g. `tokens.json`). Tooling generates platform-specific outputs (CSS variables, Swift constants, etc.). Multiple SoTs are rejected.
Theme variants (light/dark) modify aliases, NOT bases. Theming bases is rejected.
rule.rationale: Bases are universal; alias remaps are the theme contract.

rule.statement: Each token carries a short description + usage example. Bare token lists are rejected.

When the decision tree routes to `skip-this-methodology`, the agent MUST emit a structured `skip` decision (with reason + suggested-sibling) rather than running Design Tokens Basics under degraded conditions.
rule.rationale: decision-tree contract. The decision tree is the only place that knows the routing; skipping must be observable so the orchestrator can log the routed-elsewhere path.
